1→# Phase 2: Zero — Build the Prospect Intelligence Framework
     2→
     3→**Who:** <200 outbound, no CRM. Starting from scratch.
     4→**Data source:** All intelligence comes from external research. No email history to learn from.
     5→**Philosophy:** Nurge does the homework. User validates.
     6→
     7→## Resume Keys
     8→
     9→| Config key exists | Skip step |
    10→|-------------------|-----------|
    11→| `sending.domain_type` | Z1 |
    12→| `sending.deliverability_checked` | Z2 |
    13→| `prospects.geography` | Z3 |
    14→| `qualification.disqualifiers` | S1 |
    15→| `icp.company_size` | S2 |
    16→| `personas` | S3 |
    17→| `framework.transformations_seeded` | S4 |
    18→| `framework.signals_seeded` | S5 |
    19→| `framework.insights_seeded`  | S6 |
    20→| `framework.voice_seeded` | S7 |
    21→| Skills exist in `.claude/skills/nurge-*/` | S9 |
    22→| `setup_completed` | S10 |
    23→
    24→---
    25→
    26→## Z1: Sending Infrastructure
    27→
    28→Ask: "Are you sending from a dedicated outbound domain, or your main business domain?"
    29→Ask: "How many email accounts are you sending from?"
    30→Ask: "Have those been warmed up, or are they fresh?"
    31→
    32→Save:
    33→- `nurge config set sending.domain_type "<dedicated|primary>"`
    34→- `nurge config set sending.mailbox_count <number>`
    35→- `nurge config set sending.warmup_status "<warmed|fresh>"`
    36→
    37→Set limits: Fresh = 5/day. Primary domain = warn about risk. Warmed + dedicated = 30/mailbox/day.
    38→
    39→---
    40→
    41→## Z2: Deliverability Check
    42→
    43→Run `dig TXT <domain>` for SPF, DKIM, DMARC. Flag issues with fix instructions.
    44→
    45→Save: `nurge config set sending.deliverability_checked true`
    46→
    47→---
    48→
    49→## Z3: Prospect Geography
    50→
    51→Ask: "Where are your prospects mostly located?" (US / EU / Global / specific)
    52→
    53→Save: `nurge config set prospects.geography "<region>"`
    54→
    55→---
    56→
    57→## S1: Disqualifiers — "Who should I NOT waste your time on?"
    58→
    59→Nurge proposes based on product + ICP signals from website (F2):
    60→
    61→"Based on your product, I'd skip:
    62→- Companies under [X] people (no budget)
    63→- Companies over [Y] (enterprise sales cycle you can't support)
    64→- [Industry] companies (not your market)
    65→- Companies already using [product category] (unless switching)
    66→
    67→What else should I skip?"
    68→
    69→User tweaks. Save: `nurge config set qualification.disqualifiers '<json array>'`
    70→
    71→---
    72→
    73→## S2: ICP — "Who IS worth your time?"
    74→
    75→**Zero maturity: propose from website + LinkedIn research.**
    76→
    77→Use website data from F2 (product, customers mentioned, positioning).
    78→Use LinkedIn: `nurge enrich search --keywords "<product category>"` to see what kinds of companies exist in this space.
    79→
    80→"Based on your website, you sell [product] to [market]. Companies worth emailing:
    81→- [Size] employees
    82→- [Industry/vertical]
    83→- [Stage: startup/growth/enterprise]
    84→- [Geography from Z3]
    85→
    86→Sound right? Let's start narrow -- one segment."
    87→
    88→Save: `nurge config set icp.company_size "<range>"`, `icp.industry`, `icp.stage`, `icp.geography`
    89→
    90→---
    91→
    92→## S3: Personas + Buying Committee — "Who do you talk to, and who else gets involved?"
    93→
    94→**PART A: Primary personas**
    95→
    96→Nurge proposes 2-3 personas from website + product type:
    97→
    98→"Within those companies, your buyers are probably:
    99→1. [Title] -- [why they care about your product]
   100→2. [Title] -- [why they care]
   101→3. [Title] -- [why they care]
   102→
   103→Right? Anyone missing?"
   104→
   105→**PART B: Buying committee**
   106→
   107→Zero maturity has no email CC patterns to analyze. Ask directly:
   108→
   109→"When someone buys your product, who else gets involved?
   110→a) Just the person I email decides alone
   111→b) A technical person evaluates
   112→c) Their boss approves
   113→d) Procurement gets involved
   114→e) Not sure yet -- I'll learn as we go"
   115→
   116→Based on answer, set committee pattern:
   117→
   118→- (a) → single_decision_maker
   119→- (b) → champion + evaluator
   120→- (c) → champion + approver
   121→- (d) → champion + evaluator + approver + procurement
   122→- (e) → champion_only (update later from email CC patterns)
   123→
   124→Save: `nurge config set personas '<json array with committee_role per persona>'`
   125→
   126→---
   127→
   128→## S4: Transformation Models — "What makes each buyer care?"
   129→
   130→**For EACH persona (2-3 max), Nurge does external research:**
   131→
   132→1. LinkedIn research:
   133→   `nurge enrich search --keywords "<persona title> <icp industry>"` -- find 20-30 real people
   134→   Look at their headlines, what they call themselves, what companies they're at.
   135→
   136→2. Competitor/market research:
   137→   If G2 URL found in F2 links: WebFetch the G2 page for competitor reviews, complaints, praises.
   138→   If no G2 link: WebSearch "[product category] G2 reviews complaints"
   139→
   140→3. Industry pain research:
   141→   WebSearch "[persona title] biggest challenge [industry] 2026"
   142→
   143→4. Propose transformation model:
   144→
   145→   "Here's what I found about [Persona] at your ICP companies:
   146→   - [Pain point 1 from research]
   147→   - [Pain point 2]
   148→   - [What tools they currently use]
   149→
   150→   Your product changes:
   151→   - Current reality: '[what they do today without your product]'
   152→   - What changes: '[specific outcome with your product]'
   153→   - Who's affected: '[role on their team]'
   154→   - Where it fits: '[alongside/replaces what tool or process]'
   155→
   156→   Hit close?"
   157→
   158→   User validates or tweaks.
   159→
   160→5. Store per persona:
   161→   `echo '<transformation json>' | nurge knowledge seed --atom transformation --scope "<persona title>"`
   162→
   163→Save: `nurge config set framework.transformations_seeded true`
   164→
   165→---
   166→
   167→## S5: Timing Signals — "How do I know they're ready RIGHT NOW?"
   168→
   169→Nurge proposes signals based on persona research from S4:
   170→
   171→"Not every company matching your ICP is ready today. Here's what tells me the timing is right:
   172→
   173→For [Persona 1]:
   174→1. [Signal] -- [why it matters] -- I'll check [source]
   175→2. [Signal] -- [why it matters] -- I'll check [source]
   176→3. [Signal] -- [why it matters] -- I'll check [source]
   177→
   178→For [Persona 2]:
   179→1. [Signal] -- ...
   180→..."
   181→
   182→Common signals to propose (pick what's relevant):
   183→- **hiring_role**: Hiring people in the department your product serves
   184→- **team_turnover**: People leaving (from LinkedIn past_company search)
   185→- **recently_funded**: New money = new budget
   186→- **competitor_user**: Using a competitor (from job posts or G2)
   187→- **tech_stack_change**: Adopting new tech (from GitHub, job posts)
   188→- **small_gtm_team**: GTM headcount vs company size mismatch
   189→- **community_traction**: OSS stars or HN mentions without GTM to match
   190→- **leadership_change**: New VP/CTO/Head of (from LinkedIn or news)
   191→
   192→For each signal, define the check order (cheapest source first):
   193→
   194→```json
   195→{
   196→  "name": "hiring_role",
   197→  "check_order": ["website_careers", "linkedin_headlines"],
   198→  "stale_days": 14,
   199→  "applies_to": ["VP Sales"]
   200→}
   201→```
   202→
   203→Save:
   204→`echo '<signals json>' | nurge knowledge seed --atom signals --scope global`
   205→`nurge config set framework.signals_seeded true`
   206→
   207→---
   208→
   209→## S6: Insight Discovery — "What makes you impossible to ignore?"
   210→
   211→Nurge explains the data-first approach:
   212→
   213→"When I research a prospect, I don't follow a checklist. I gather everything available, read all of it, and find the thing they don't expect you to know. The insight that stands out depends on the person, not a formula.
   214→
   215→Here's what I look at:
   216→- LinkedIn: team changes, hiring, departures, role shifts
   217→- Job posts: tools mentioned, skills they're hiring for
   218→- Website: product changes, positioning shifts, new pages
   219→- Funding/news: raises, pivots, acquisitions
   220→- GitHub/community: tech stack, open source activity, HN mentions
   221→- G2/reviews: competitor usage, complaints, switching signals
   222→
   223→The unique insight emerges from the data. Sometimes it's a team change. Sometimes it's a job post that contradicts their current stack. Sometimes it's a funding round nobody connected to their hiring spree yet.
   224→
   225→I'll read everything, then pick the one thing that makes this email impossible to ignore."
   226→
   227→User may add sources or flag what they've found works: "My best replies came from noticing job posts."
   228→
   229→Save:
   230→`echo '<insight_approach json>' | nurge knowledge seed --atom insight_approach --scope global`
   231→`nurge config set framework.insights_seeded true`
   232→
   233→---
   234→
   235→## S7: Voice + Positioning
   236→
   237→**Positioning:** Already have it from F2 website scrape. Present a one-liner for cold emails:
   238→
   239→"[Name], for cold emails I'd lead with: '[positioning]'. Sound right?"
   240→
   241→Iterate until it clicks. Under 15 words, benefit-focused.
   242→
   243→Save: `nurge config set product.positioning "<one-liner>"`
   244→
   245→**Social proof:** Check website data from F2. Present any proof points found. Ask to add/remove.
   246→
   247→Save: `nurge config set product.social_proof '<json or none>'`
   248→
   249→**Voice:** Zero maturity has no emails to extract from. Ask:
   250→
   251→"How do you usually write to prospects?
   252→a) Casual, like texting a friend
   253→b) Professional but warm
   254→c) Direct, no fluff
   255→d) Show me an example you like"
   256→
   257→If (d): user provides an example, extract tone from it.
   258→
   259→Seed baseline voice:
   260→`echo '<tone json>' | nurge knowledge seed --atom tone --scope global`
   261→
   262→Save: `nurge config set framework.voice_seeded true`
   263→
   264→---
   265→
   266→## S8: KPIs & Goals
   267→
   268→Ask: "What's your main outbound goal?" (Book meetings / Build pipeline / Validate PMF)
   269→
   270→Suggest conservative KPIs based on warmup status from Z1:
   271→- Fresh: 5 emails/day, 1-2 replies/week, 1 meeting/week
   272→- Warmed: 20-30/day, 5-10% reply rate, 2-3 meetings/week
   273→
   274→Save: `nurge config set kpi.goal`, `kpi.daily_emails`, `kpi.weekly_meetings`, `kpi.reply_rate_target`
   275→
   276→---
   277→
   278→## S9: Seed Framework + Install Skills
   279→
   280→**Step A: Seed the prospect intelligence framework atom.**
   281→
   282→Combine everything from S1-S7 into one framework:
   283→
   284→```bash
   285→echo '{
   286→  "qualification": {
   287→    "icp": { ... from S2 ... },
   288→    "disqualifiers": [ ... from S1 ... ],
   289→    "must_have_signal": true
   290→  },
   291→  "buying_committee": { ... from S3 ... },
   292→  "personas": {
   293→    "<title>": {
   294→      "committee_role": "...",
   295→      "transformation": { ... from S4 ... },
   296→      "timing_signals": [ ... from S5 ... ],
   297→      "insight_approach": "data-first -- gather all signals, then surface what's unique"
   298→    }
   299→  },
   300→  "signals": { ... from S5 ... },
   301→  "voice": { ... from S7 ... }
   302→}' | nurge knowledge seed --atom prospect_intelligence_framework --scope global
   303→```
   304→
   305→**Step B: Seed remaining knowledge atoms** (hooks, positioning, compliance, structure):
   306→
   307→```bash
   308→echo '{"primary":"...","proof_points":[...]}' | nurge knowledge seed --atom positioning --scope global
   309→echo '{"word_limit":[50,90],"sections":["hook","value","cta"]}' | nurge knowledge seed --atom structure --scope first_touch
   310→echo '{"requirements":["..."]}' | nurge knowledge seed --atom compliance --scope "<region>"
   311→```
   312→
   313→**Step C: Install thin skill wrappers.**
   314→
   315→```bash
   316→nurge skills install-wrappers --force
   317→```
   318→
   319→**Step D: Score existing contacts (if any).**
   320→
   321→```bash
   322→nurge tier --batch --limit 100
   323→```
   324→
   325→---
   326→
   327→## S10: Live Test — Full Framework Execution
   328→
   329→Don't announce "this is a demo." Just do it.
   330→
   331→1. Brief recap (2-3 lines): "[Name], you're set up: [product], targeting [ICP], reaching [personas]."
   332→
   333→2. Ask: "Give me a company you'd love to sell to."
   334→
   335→3. Run the complete prospect intelligence framework:
   336→
   337→   a) WebFetch {company website}
   338→      -> Product info + extract social links (LinkedIn, GitHub, G2, blog)
   339→
   340→   b) `nurge enrich company {linkedin_slug}` (from link in step a)
   341→      -> Company profile + ALL employees
   342→      -> Scan employees: map to buying committee roles
   343→      -> Check hiring signals from headlines
   344→
   345→   c) `nurge enrich person {decision_maker}` (champion from committee mapping)
   346→      -> Full profile, followers, hashtags
   347→
   348→   d) `nurge enrich github {org}` (if GitHub link found)
   349→      -> Repos, tech stack, stars
   350→
   351→   e) `nurge enrich hn {company}`
   352→      -> Community mentions, sentiment
   353→
   354→   f) Check timing signals from signal playbook:
   355→      -> Run each signal's check_order against data already collected
   356→      -> WebSearch only for gaps (funding, etc.)
   357→
   358→   g) Read ALL collected data from steps a-f. Find the one insight this person
   359→      wouldn't expect you to know. Don't follow a priority list -- the unique
   360→      insight emerges from what the data actually says about THIS person.
   361→
   362→   h) Assemble dossier + draft email:
   363→      -> Signal + insight + transformation model + voice + buying committee names
   364→      -> 50-90 words
   365→
   366→4. Present: "[Name], here's your first outbound email ([X] words):"
   367→
   368→5. Ask: "Want to send it, or should I adjust?"
   369→
   370→6. Mark complete: `nurge config set setup_completed true`
   371→
   372→7. Next steps:
   373→   - "/nurge:outbound -- write emails"
   374→   - "/nurge:research -- deep-dive a company"

file.content:
# Phase 2: Zero — Build the Prospect Intelligence Framework

**Who:** <200 outbound, no CRM. Starting from scratch.
**Data source:** All intelligence comes from external research. No email history to learn from.
**Philosophy:** Nurge does the homework. User validates.

## Resume Keys

| Config key exists | Skip step |
|-------------------|-----------|
| `sending.domain_type` | Z1 |
| `sending.deliverability_checked` | Z2 |
| `prospects.geography` | Z3 |
| `qualification.disqualifiers` | S1 |
| `icp.company_size` | S2 |
| `personas` | S3 |
| `framework.transformations_seeded` | S4 |
| `framework.signals_seeded` | S5 |
| `framework.insights_seeded`  | S6 |
| `framework.voice_seeded` | S7 |
| Skills exist in `.claude/skills/nurge-*/` | S9 |
| `setup_completed` | S10 |

---

## Z1: Sending Infrastructure

Ask: "Are you sending from a dedicated outbound domain, or your main business domain?"
Ask: "How many email accounts are you sending from?"
Ask: "Have those been warmed up, or are they fresh?"

Save:
- `nurge config set sending.domain_type "<dedicated|primary>"`
- `nurge config set sending.mailbox_count <number>`
- `nurge config set sending.warmup_status "<warmed|fresh>"`

Set limits: Fresh = 5/day. Primary domain = warn about risk. Warmed + dedicated = 30/mailbox/day.

---

## Z2: Deliverability Check

Run `dig TXT <domain>` for SPF, DKIM, DMARC. Flag issues with fix instructions.

Save: `nurge config set sending.deliverability_checked true`

---

## Z3: Prospect Geography

Ask: "Where are your prospects mostly located?" (US / EU / Global / specific)

Save: `nurge config set prospects.geography "<region>"`

---

## S1: Disqualifiers — "Who should I NOT waste your time on?"

Nurge proposes based on product + ICP signals from website (F2):

"Based on your product, I'd skip:
- Companies under [X] people (no budget)
- Companies over [Y] (enterprise sales cycle you can't support)
- [Industry] companies (not your market)
- Companies already using [product category] (unless switching)

What else should I skip?"

User tweaks. Save: `nurge config set qualification.disqualifiers '<json array>'`

---

## S2: ICP — "Who IS worth your time?"

**Zero maturity: propose from website + LinkedIn research.**

Use website data from F2 (product, customers mentioned, positioning).
Use LinkedIn: `nurge enrich search --keywords "<product category>"` to see what kinds of companies exist in this space.

"Based on your website, you sell [product] to [market]. Companies worth emailing:
- [Size] employees
- [Industry/vertical]
- [Stage: startup/growth/enterprise]
- [Geography from Z3]

Sound right? Let's start narrow -- one segment."

Save: `nurge config set icp.company_size "<range>"`, `icp.industry`, `icp.stage`, `icp.geography`

---

## S3: Personas + Buying Committee — "Who do you talk to, and who else gets involved?"

**PART A: Primary personas**

Nurge proposes 2-3 personas from website + product type:

"Within those companies, your buyers are probably:
1. [Title] -- [why they care about your product]
2. [Title] -- [why they care]
3. [Title] -- [why they care]

Right? Anyone missing?"

**PART B: Buying committee**

Zero maturity has no email CC patterns to analyze. Ask directly:

"When someone buys your product, who else gets involved?
a) Just the person I email decides alone
b) A technical person evaluates
c) Their boss approves
d) Procurement gets involved
e) Not sure yet -- I'll learn as we go"

Based on answer, set committee pattern:

- (a) → single_decision_maker
- (b) → champion + evaluator
- (c) → champion + approver
- (d) → champion + evaluator + approver + procurement
- (e) → champion_only (update later from email CC patterns)

Save: `nurge config set personas '<json array with committee_role per persona>'`

---

## S4: Transformation Models — "What makes each buyer care?"

**For EACH persona (2-3 max), Nurge does external research:**

1. LinkedIn research:
   `nurge enrich search --keywords "<persona title> <icp industry>"` -- find 20-30 real people
   Look at their headlines, what they call themselves, what companies they're at.

2. Competitor/market research:
   If G2 URL found in F2 links: WebFetch the G2 page for competitor reviews, complaints, praises.
   If no G2 link: WebSearch "[product category] G2 reviews complaints"

3. Industry pain research:
   WebSearch "[persona title] biggest challenge [industry] 2026"

4. Propose transformation model:

   "Here's what I found about [Persona] at your ICP companies:
   - [Pain point 1 from research]
   - [Pain point 2]
   - [What tools they currently use]

   Your product changes:
   - Current reality: '[what they do today without your product]'
   - What changes: '[specific outcome with your product]'
   - Who's affected: '[role on their team]'
   - Where it fits: '[alongside/replaces what tool or process]'

   Hit close?"

   User validates or tweaks.

5. Store per persona:
   `echo '<transformation json>' | nurge knowledge seed --atom transformation --scope "<persona title>"`

Save: `nurge config set framework.transformations_seeded true`

---

## S5: Timing Signals — "How do I know they're ready RIGHT NOW?"

Nurge proposes signals based on persona research from S4:

"Not every company matching your ICP is ready today. Here's what tells me the timing is right:

For [Persona 1]:
1. [Signal] -- [why it matters] -- I'll check [source]
2. [Signal] -- [why it matters] -- I'll check [source]
3. [Signal] -- [why it matters] -- I'll check [source]

For [Persona 2]:
1. [Signal] -- ...
..."

Common signals to propose (pick what's relevant):
- **hiring_role**: Hiring people in the department your product serves
- **team_turnover**: People leaving (from LinkedIn past_company search)
- **recently_funded**: New money = new budget
- **competitor_user**: Using a competitor (from job posts or G2)
- **tech_stack_change**: Adopting new tech (from GitHub, job posts)
- **small_gtm_team**: GTM headcount vs company size mismatch
- **community_traction**: OSS stars or HN mentions without GTM to match
- **leadership_change**: New VP/CTO/Head of (from LinkedIn or news)

For each signal, define the check order (cheapest source first):

```json
{
  "name": "hiring_role",
  "check_order": ["website_careers", "linkedin_headlines"],
  "stale_days": 14,
  "applies_to": ["VP Sales"]
}
```

Save:
`echo '<signals json>' | nurge knowledge seed --atom signals --scope global`
`nurge config set framework.signals_seeded true`

---

## S6: Insight Discovery — "What makes you impossible to ignore?"

Nurge explains the data-first approach:

"When I research a prospect, I don't follow a checklist. I gather everything available, read all of it, and find the thing they don't expect you to know. The insight that stands out depends on the person, not a formula.

Here's what I look at:
- LinkedIn: team changes, hiring, departures, role shifts
- Job posts: tools mentioned, skills they're hiring for
- Website: product changes, positioning shifts, new pages
- Funding/news: raises, pivots, acquisitions
- GitHub/community: tech stack, open source activity, HN mentions
- G2/reviews: competitor usage, complaints, switching signals

The unique insight emerges from the data. Sometimes it's a team change. Sometimes it's a job post that contradicts their current stack. Sometimes it's a funding round nobody connected to their hiring spree yet.

I'll read everything, then pick the one thing that makes this email impossible to ignore."

User may add sources or flag what they've found works: "My best replies came from noticing job posts."

Save:
`echo '<insight_approach json>' | nurge knowledge seed --atom insight_approach --scope global`
`nurge config set framework.insights_seeded true`

---

## S7: Voice + Positioning

**Positioning:** Already have it from F2 website scrape. Present a one-liner for cold emails:

"[Name], for cold emails I'd lead with: '[positioning]'. Sound right?"

Iterate until it clicks. Under 15 words, benefit-focused.

Save: `nurge config set product.positioning "<one-liner>"`

**Social proof:** Check website data from F2. Present any proof points found. Ask to add/remove.

Save: `nurge config set product.social_proof '<json or none>'`

**Voice:** Zero maturity has no emails to extract from. Ask:

"How do you usually write to prospects?
a) Casual, like texting a friend
b) Professional but warm
c) Direct, no fluff
d) Show me an example you like"

If (d): user provides an example, extract tone from it.

Seed baseline voice:
`echo '<tone json>' | nurge knowledge seed --atom tone --scope global`

Save: `nurge config set framework.voice_seeded true`

---

## S8: KPIs & Goals

Ask: "What's your main outbound goal?" (Book meetings / Build pipeline / Validate PMF)

Suggest conservative KPIs based on warmup status from Z1:
- Fresh: 5 emails/day, 1-2 replies/week, 1 meeting/week
- Warmed: 20-30/day, 5-10% reply rate, 2-3 meetings/week

Save: `nurge config set kpi.goal`, `kpi.daily_emails`, `kpi.weekly_meetings`, `kpi.reply_rate_target`

---

## S9: Seed Framework + Install Skills

**Step A: Seed the prospect intelligence framework atom.**

Combine everything from S1-S7 into one framework:

```bash
echo '{
  "qualification": {
    "icp": { ... from S2 ... },
    "disqualifiers": [ ... from S1 ... ],
    "must_have_signal": true
  },
  "buying_committee": { ... from S3 ... },
  "personas": {
    "<title>": {
      "committee_role": "...",
      "transformation": { ... from S4 ... },
      "timing_signals": [ ... from S5 ... ],
      "insight_approach": "data-first -- gather all signals, then surface what's unique"
    }
  },
  "signals": { ... from S5 ... },
  "voice": { ... from S7 ... }
}' | nurge knowledge seed --atom prospect_intelligence_framework --scope global
```

**Step B: Seed remaining knowledge atoms** (hooks, positioning, compliance, structure):

```bash
echo '{"primary":"...","proof_points":[...]}' | nurge knowledge seed --atom positioning --scope global
echo '{"word_limit":[50,90],"sections":["hook","value","cta"]}' | nurge knowledge seed --atom structure --scope first_touch
echo '{"requirements":["..."]}' | nurge knowledge seed --atom compliance --scope "<region>"
```

**Step C: Install thin skill wrappers.**

```bash
nurge skills install-wrappers --force
```

**Step D: Score existing contacts (if any).**

```bash
nurge tier --batch --limit 100
```

---

## S10: Live Test — Full Framework Execution

Don't announce "this is a demo." Just do it.

1. Brief recap (2-3 lines): "[Name], you're set up: [product], targeting [ICP], reaching [personas]."

2. Ask: "Give me a company you'd love to sell to."

3. Run the complete prospect intelligence framework:

   a) WebFetch {company website}
      -> Product info + extract social links (LinkedIn, GitHub, G2, blog)

   b) `nurge enrich company {linkedin_slug}` (from link in step a)
      -> Company profile + ALL employees
      -> Scan employees: map to buying committee roles
      -> Check hiring signals from headlines

   c) `nurge enrich person {decision_maker}` (champion from committee mapping)
      -> Full profile, followers, hashtags

   d) `nurge enrich github {org}` (if GitHub link found)
      -> Repos, tech stack, stars

   e) `nurge enrich hn {company}`
      -> Community mentions, sentiment

   f) Check timing signals from signal playbook:
      -> Run each signal's check_order against data already collected
      -> WebSearch only for gaps (funding, etc.)

   g) Read ALL collected data from steps a-f. Find the one insight this person
      wouldn't expect you to know. Don't follow a priority list -- the unique
      insight emerges from what the data actually says about THIS person.

   h) Assemble dossier + draft email:
      -> Signal + insight + transformation model + voice + buying committee names
      -> 50-90 words

4. Present: "[Name], here's your first outbound email ([X] words):"

5. Ask: "Want to send it, or should I adjust?"

6. Mark complete: `nurge config set setup_completed true`

7. Next steps:
   - "/nurge:outbound -- write emails"
   - "/nurge:research -- deep-dive a company"
   - "/nurge:pipeline -- see what's next"

content.text: Now I see the gap. Let me check if there are other phase-2 files to understand the full branching setup.

content.content:
internal/project/skills/setup/phase-2-experimenting.md
internal/project/skills/setup/phase-2-operating.md
internal/project/skills/setup/phase-2-scaling.md
internal/project/skills/setup/phase-2-zero.md

content.text:
Here's the core tension:

**process.md says:**
- No separate knowledge atoms to seed. Skills generated from one unified profile.
- 1 parameterized flow, not 4 branching files
- ~5 confirmations + 2 real questions (down from 30+ config fields)
- Phase 2 is "Here's What I Learned" - Claude reads emails, presents findings, user confirms

**Current setup has:**
- 12 atom types seeded individually through explicit CLI commands
- 4 separate phase-2 files (zero, experimenting, operating, scaling) with duplicated S-steps
- phase-2-zero alone has 10 steps (Z1-Z3, S1-S10) with many questions

Three things need to change:

### 1. Collapse 4 files into 1 parameterized flow

Instead of branching to zero/experimenting/operating/scaling, one flow that adapts based on what data is available:

- **Has email history?** Claude reads batches, derives tone/ICP/personas/positioning/signals, presents "Here's What I Learned" for confirmation
- **No email history?** Falls back to website research + the few questions that can't be derived (anti-ICP, goals, what they want to change)

The maturity level determines how much can be derived vs asked - it's a parameter, not a separate file.

### 2. The atom types become internal, not user-facing steps

The 12 atom types in `knowledge.go` are fine as **internal storage granularity**. The problem is that setup explicitly seeds them one by one (S4: seed transformation, S5: seed signals, S6: seed insight_approach, S7: seed tone...). 

Instead: Claude's analysis of email batches produces one unified context object. The Go binary's `nurge context generate` (or a new `nurge context assemble`) decomposes it into atoms internally. Setup never calls `nurge knowledge seed` directly - it calls one command that takes Claude's full analysis and stores it however it wants.

### 3. Some atom types may be unnecessary

process.md's "one unified profile" suggests the atom granularity could simplify:

| Current atoms | process.md equivalent |
|---|---|
| tone, hooks, structure | **voice** (extracted from actual emails, not dropdown + rigid rules) |
| positioning, transformation | **product** (what you sell + what changes for each persona) |
| personas, objections | **buyers** (who + how they push back) |
| timing, signals | **signals** (when to reach out) |
| compliance | stays (geography-dependent) |
| prospect_intelligence_framework | this IS the unified profile - but currently it duplicates everything else |
| insight_approach | not an atom - it's a principle ("data first, find what's unique") baked into the outbound skill |

The `prospect_intelligence_framework` atom is already trying to be the unified profile (S9 combines everything into it), but then S9 also seeds positioning/structure/compliance separately - that's the redundancy process.md flagged.

### What this means for `knowledge.go`

The atom types could shrink to ~5 (voice, product, buyers, signals, compliance) with the unified profile being the primary assembly output, not a separate atom. Or keep the current types but make `nurge context assemble` the only writer - setup never seeds atoms directly.

Want me to draft the unified phase-2 flow, or start with refactoring the atom types?

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/rejoesudarsan/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/5.0.6/skills/brainstorming

# Brainstorming Ideas Into Designs

Help turn ideas into fully formed designs and specs through natural collaborative dialogue.

Start by understanding the current project context, then ask questions one at a time to refine the idea. Once you understand what you're building, present the design and get user approval.

<HARD-GATE>
Do NOT invoke any implementation skill, write any code, scaffold any project, or take any implementation action until you have presented a design and the user has approved it. This applies to EVERY project regardless of perceived simplicity.
</HARD-GATE>

## Anti-Pattern: "This Is Too Simple To Need A Design"

Every project goes through this process. A todo list, a single-function utility, a config change — all of them. "Simple" projects are where unexamined assumptions cause the most wasted work. The design can be short (a few sentences for truly simple projects), but you MUST present it and get approval.

## Checklist

You MUST create a task for each of these items and complete them in order:

1. **Explore project context** — check files, docs, recent commits
2. **Offer visual companion** (if topic will involve visual questions) — this is its own message, not combined with a clarifying question. See the Visual Companion section below.
3. **Ask clarifying questions** — one at a time, understand purpose/constraints/success criteria
4. **Propose 2-3 approaches** — with trade-offs and your recommendation
5. **Present design** — in sections scaled to their complexity, get user approval after each section
6. **Write design doc** — save to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md` and commit
7. **Spec self-review** — quick inline check for placeholders, contradictions, ambiguity, scope (see below)
8. **User reviews written spec** — ask user to review the spec file before proceeding
9. **Transition to implementation** — invoke writing-plans skill to create implementation plan

## Process Flow

```dot
digraph brainstorming {
    "Explore project context" [shape=box];
    "Visual questions ahead?" [shape=diamond];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [shape=box];
    "Ask clarifying questions" [shape=box];
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" [shape=box];
    "Present design sections" [shape=box];
    "User approves design?" [shape=diamond];
    "Write design doc" [shape=box];
    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" [shape=box];
    "User reviews spec?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke writing-plans skill" [shape=doublecircle];

    "Explore project context" -> "Visual questions ahead?";
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [label="yes"];
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Ask clarifying questions" [label="no"];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" -> "Ask clarifying questions";
    "Ask clarifying questions" -> "Propose 2-3 approaches";
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" -> "Present design sections";
    "Present design sections" -> "User approves design?";
    "User approves design?" -> "Present design sections" [label="no, revise"];
    "User approves design?" -> "Write design doc" [label="yes"];
    "Write design doc" -> "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)";
    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" -> "User reviews spec?";
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Write design doc" [label="changes requested"];
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Invoke writing-plans skill" [label="approved"];
}
```

**The terminal state is invoking writing-plans.** Do NOT invoke frontend-design, mcp-builder, or any other implementation skill. The ONLY skill you invoke after brainstorming is writing-plans.

## The Process

**Understanding the idea:**

- Check out the current project state first (files, docs, recent commits)
- Before asking detailed questions, assess scope: if the request describes multiple independent subsystems (e.g., "build a platform with chat, file storage, billing, and analytics"), flag this immediately. Don't spend questions refining details of a project that needs to be decomposed first.
- If the project is too large for a single spec, help the user decompose into sub-projects: what are the independent pieces, how do they relate, what order should they be built? Then brainstorm the first sub-project through the normal design flow. Each sub-project gets its own spec → plan → implementation cycle.
- For appropriately-scoped projects, ask questions one at a time to refine the idea
- Prefer multiple choice questions when possible, but open-ended is fine too
- Only one question per message - if a topic needs more exploration, break it into multiple questions
- Focus on understanding: purpose, constraints, success criteria

**Exploring approaches:**

- Propose 2-3 different approaches with trade-offs
- Present options conversationally with your recommendation and reasoning
- Lead with your recommended option and explain why

**Presenting the design:**

- Once you believe you understand what you're building, present the design
- Scale each section to its complexity: a few sentences if straightforward, up to 200-300 words if nuanced
- Ask after each section whether it looks right so far
- Cover: architecture, components, data flow, error handling, testing
- Be ready to go back and clarify if something doesn't make sense

**Design for isolation and clarity:**

- Break the system into smaller units that each have one clear purpose, communicate through well-defined interfaces, and can be understood and tested independently
- For each unit, you should be able to answer: what does it do, how do you use it, and what does it depend on?
- Can someone understand what a unit does without reading its internals? Can you change the internals without breaking consumers? If not, the boundaries need work.
- Smaller, well-bounded units are also easier for you to work with - you reason better about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. When a file grows large, that's often a signal that it's doing too much.

**Working in existing codebases:**

- Explore the current structure before proposing changes. Follow existing patterns.
- Where existing code has problems that affect the work (e.g., a file that's grown too large, unclear boundaries, tangled responsibilities), include targeted improvements as part of the design - the way a good developer improves code they're working in.
- Don't propose unrelated refactoring. Stay focused on what serves the current goal.

## After the Design

**Documentation:**

- Write the validated design (spec) to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md`
  - (User preferences for spec location override this default)
- Use elements-of-style:writing-clearly-and-concisely skill if available
- Commit the design document to git

**Spec Self-Review:**
After writing the spec document, look at it with fresh eyes:

1. **Placeholder scan:** Any "TBD", "TODO", incomplete sections, or vague requirements? Fix them.
2. **Internal consistency:** Do any sections contradict each other? Does the architecture match the feature descriptions?
3. **Scope check:** Is this focused enough for a single implementation plan, or does it need decomposition?
4. **Ambiguity check:** Could any requirement be interpreted two different ways? If so, pick one and make it explicit.

Fix any issues inline. No need to re-review — just fix and move on.

**User Review Gate:**
After the spec review loop passes, ask the user to review the written spec before proceeding:

> "Spec written and committed to `<path>`. Please review it and let me know if you want to make any changes before we start writing out the implementation plan."

Wait for the user's response. If they request changes, make them and re-run the spec review loop. Only proceed once the user approves.

**Implementation:**

- Invoke the writing-plans skill to create a detailed implementation plan
- Do NOT invoke any other skill. writing-plans is the next step.

## Key Principles

- **One question at a time** - Don't overwhelm with multiple questions
- **Multiple choice preferred** - Easier to answer than open-ended when possible
- **YAGNI ruthlessly** - Remove unnecessary features from all designs
- **Explore alternatives** - Always propose 2-3 approaches before settling
- **Incremental validation** - Present design, get approval before moving on
- **Be flexible** - Go back and clarify when something doesn't make sense

## Visual Companion

A browser-based companion for showing mockups, diagrams, and visual options during brainstorming. Available as a tool — not a mode. Accepting the companion means it's available for questions that benefit from visual treatment; it does NOT mean every question goes through the browser.

**Offering the companion:** When you anticipate that upcoming questions will involve visual content (mockups, layouts, diagrams), offer it once for consent:
> "Some of what we're working on might be easier to explain if I can show it to you in a web browser. I can put together mockups, diagrams, comparisons, and other visuals as we go. This feature is still new and can be token-intensive. Want to try it? (Requires opening a local URL)"

**This offer MUST be its own message.** Do not combine it with clarifying questions, context summaries, or any other content. The message should contain ONLY the offer above and nothing else. Wait for the user's response before continuing. If they decline, proceed with text-only brainstorming.

**Per-question decision:** Even after the user accepts, decide FOR EACH QUESTION whether to use the browser or the terminal. The test: **would the user understand this better by seeing it than reading it?**

- **Use the browser** for content that IS visual — mockups, wireframes, layout comparisons, architecture diagrams, side-by-side visual designs
- **Use the terminal** for content that is text — requirements questions, conceptual choices, tradeoff lists, A/B/C/D text options, scope decisions

A question about a UI topic is not automatically a visual question. "What does personality mean in this context?" is a conceptual question — use the terminal. "Which wizard layout works better?" is a visual question — use the browser.

If they agree to the companion, read the detailed guide before proceeding:
`skills/brainstorming/visual-companion.md`

1→# Phase 2: Experimenting — Build the Prospect Intelligence Framework
     2→
     3→**Who:** 200-499 outbound, or <200 with CRM connected. Some data exists.
     4→**Data source:** Hybrid -- email patterns + external research fill gaps.
     5→**Philosophy:** Learn from what exists, research what's missing.
     6→
     7→## Resume Keys
     8→
     9→Same as phase-2-zero.md (S1-S10 steps use identical config keys).
    10→
    11→## Pre-Step: Present Email Insights
    12→
    13→Before starting the intelligence build, show what the email data reveals:
    14→
    15→"[Name], I classified your emails. Here's what I see:
    16→- [X] outbound emails in the last 90 days
    17→- Top companies you email: [list top 5]
    18→- Top titles you reach: [list top 3 titles]
    19→- Reply patterns: [any visible patterns]
    20→
    21→Let me use this to build your intelligence framework."

# Phase 2: Experimenting — Build the Prospect Intelligence Framework

**Who:** 200-499 outbound, or <200 with CRM connected. Some data exists.
**Data source:** Hybrid -- email patterns + external research fill gaps.
**Philosophy:** Learn from what exists, research what's missing.

## Resume Keys

Same as phase-2-zero.md (S1-S10 steps use identical config keys).

## Pre-Step: Present Email Insights

Before starting the intelligence build, show what the email data reveals:

"[Name], I classified your emails. Here's what I see:
- [X] outbound emails in the last 90 days
- Top companies you email: [list top 5]
- Top titles you reach: [list top 3 titles]
- Reply patterns: [any visible patterns]

Let me use this to build your intelligence framework."

---

## How Experimenting Differs From Zero

The S1-S10 steps follow the **same structure** as phase-2-zero.md. Read that file for the full step definitions. Below are ONLY the differences in data sources:

### S1: Disqualifiers
Same as zero. Propose from website + email patterns.
EXTRA: "From your emails, I can see you never email [industry/size]. Should we formalize that as a skip?"

### S2: ICP
**FROM EMAILS:** Analyze classified outbound to find who they actually email.
"Based on your emails, you mostly reach out to:
- [Company size range] (from domain analysis)
- [Industry] (from company types)
- [Titles] (from recipient patterns)

Does this match who you WANT to target, or are there segments you're missing?"

### S3: Personas + Buying Committee
**PART A personas:** Infer from email recipient titles.
"Your emails go to these roles most: [VP Sales 34%, Founder 28%, RevOps 18%]. These are your personas."

**PART B buying committee:** Analyze CC patterns from email threads.
"From your threads:
- You email [champion role] first
- [Evaluator role] gets CC'd in [X]% of threads
- [Blocker role] joins in [X]% of threads

Your buying committee: champion -> evaluator -> blocker -> approver"

If CC data is sparse, fall back to asking (same as zero).

### S4: Transformation Models
Same research flow as zero, PLUS:
- Reference actual email performance: "Your emails that get replies mention [X] -- let me build on that."
- Transformation model blends email patterns with external research.

### S5: Timing Signals
Same as zero, PLUS:
- Calibrate with reply data: "Companies that raised funding reply at [X]% vs [Y]% average. Prioritize that."

### S6: Insight Priorities
Same as zero.

### S7: Voice + Positioning
**Voice: EXTRACTED from emails** (not picked from a list).
"Here's your writing style from [X] emails:
- Formality: [level]
- Avg words: [count]
- Opener pattern: [questions/stats/direct]
- CTA style: [soft ask/meeting request/etc]
- Anti-patterns: [things you never do]

Sound like you?"

### S8-S10: Same as zero.

# Phase 2: Operating — Build the Prospect Intelligence Framework

**Who:** 500+ outbound, CRM connected, solo or small team.
**Data source:** Data-first. Rich email history + CRM pipeline. Show them what they don't know.
**Philosophy:** Reveal hidden patterns, then add intelligence on top.

## Resume Keys

Same as phase-2-zero.md (S1-S10 steps use identical config keys).

## Pre-Step: Deep Email Analysis

With 500+ emails, present a full performance analysis before the intelligence build:

"[Name], I analyzed [X] outbound emails. Here's what the data says:

WHO REPLIES (not just who you email):
| Persona         | Emails | Reply Rate |
|-----------------|--------|------------|
| [Title 1]       | [N]    | [X]%      |
| [Title 2]       | [N]    | [X]%      |
| [Title 3]       | [N]    | [X]%      |

WHAT WORKS:
- Subject lines that get opens: [pattern]
- Openers that get replies: [pattern]
- Best CTA style: [pattern]
- Best day/time: [pattern]

SURPRISES:
- [Persona] replies at [X]% -- higher than [Persona] despite fewer emails
- Emails mentioning [topic] get [X]x more replies
- Your reply rate drops after [X] follow-ups

Let me build your intelligence framework using these patterns."

---

## How Operating Differs From Zero

The S1-S10 steps follow the **same structure** as phase-2-zero.md. Read that file for full step definitions. Below are ONLY the differences:

### S1: Disqualifiers
**DATA-DRIVEN:** "Companies in [industry/size] never reply to you (0% over [N] emails). Skip them?"

### S2: ICP
**FROM REPLY DATA, not just send data:**
"You email VP Sales the most (340 emails) but RevOps replies at 15% vs VP Sales at 12%. Your real ICP might be different from who you think it is. Want to lean into what works?"

### S3: Personas + Buying Committee
**PART A personas:** Ranked by reply rate AND deal conversion (CRM data).
"Your top 3 personas by actual conversion:
1. RevOps -- 15% reply, 2.5% deal rate
2. VP Sales -- 12% reply, 0.7% deal rate
3. Founder -- 8% reply, 0.8% deal rate

Surprise: RevOps converts 3.5x better than VP Sales."

**PART B buying committee:** Rich CC/thread data from 500+ emails.
"Your email threads show a clear buying committee pattern:
- You email [Champion] first (100% of threads)
- [Evaluator] gets CC'd after [X] exchanges ([Y]% of threads)
- [Blocker] joins in [Z]% of threads (usually asks about [topic])
- [Approver] appears in [W]% of threads (usually near close)

Average thread length before deal: [N] emails across [M] people."

### S4: Transformation Models
**DATA-PROVEN patterns:**
"For RevOps (your best-converting persona):
- Your emails that convert mention 'data hygiene' and 'pipeline reporting'
- You never pitch the product in first email -- you ask about their stack
- Replies come when you name a specific tool they use

Transformation model: 'RevOps spends 10+ hours/week cleaning CRM data.
Your product automates the cleanup at the source.'

This matches what works in your actual emails. Hit close?"

### S5: Timing Signals
**CALIBRATED by actual reply data:**
"Here's what predicts replies in YOUR data:
- Recently funded companies: 18% reply rate (vs 8% average)
- Companies with >3 SDRs: 14% reply rate
- Companies using [competitor]: 16% reply rate

I'll prioritize these signals based on your proven data, not industry averages."

### S6: Insight Priorities
Same as zero, but validated: "Your emails with people insights (naming team members) get [X]x more replies than generic ones. Confirms people insight as #1 priority."

### S7: Voice + Positioning
**Fully extracted with performance correlation:**
"Your voice profile from [X] emails:
- Formality: [X]/10
- Avg email: [X] words
- Never uses: [patterns]
- Always uses: [patterns]
- Best opener: [specific pattern with reply rate]
- Best CTA: '[exact phrase]' ([X]% reply rate vs [Y]% for alternatives)

Your best-performing email: [show it with analysis of why it worked]"

### S8-S10: Same as zero.

1→# Phase 2: Scaling — Build the Prospect Intelligence Framework
     2→
     3→**Who:** 500+ outbound, CRM connected, team >1 person.
     4→**Data source:** Cross-team email data + CRM pipeline. Team-wide intelligence.
     5→**Philosophy:** Find what each rep does best, make the whole team that good.
     6→
     7→## Resume Keys
     8→
     9→Same as phase-2-zero.md (S1-S10 steps use identical config keys).
    10→
    11→## Pre-Step: Team + Cross-Rep Analysis
    12→
    13→"[Name], I analyzed [X] emails from your connected mailbox. Your CRM shows [Y] reps on the team.
    14→
    15→TEAM PERFORMANCE:
    16→| Metric          | You     | Team Avg | Best Rep |
    17→|-----------------|---------|----------|----------|
    18→| Reply rate      | [X]%    | [Y]%     | [Z]%    |
    19→| Emails/day      | [X]     | [Y]      | [Z]     |
    20→| Meetings/week   | [X]     | [Y]      | [Z]     |
    21→
    22→WHO YOUR TEAM TARGETS vs WHO CONVERTS:
    23→| Persona         | Emails | Deals  | Win Rate   |
    24→|-----------------|--------|--------|------------|
    25→| [Title 1]       | [N]    | [N]    | [X]%       |
    26→| [Title 2]       | [N]    | [N]    | [X]%       |
    27→| [Title 3]       | [N]    | [N]    | [X]%       |
    28→
    29→CROSS-REP PATTERNS:
    30→- [Rep A] is best at [Persona] emails ([X]% win rate)
    31→- [Rep B] is best at [Persona] emails ([X]% win rate)
    32→- You are best at [Persona] ([X]% win rate)
    33→
    34→Let me build a framework that captures what your BEST rep does for each persona."
    35→
    36→**Note:** `nurge sync` only supports one Gmail mailbox. Cross-rep analysis uses CRM data (deal owner, pipeline stages) plus the connected mailbox. Multi-mailbox sync is future work.
    37→
    38→---
    39→
    40→## How Scaling Differs From Zero
    41→
    42→The S1-S10 steps follow the **same structure** as phase-2-zero.md. Read that file for full step definitions. Below are ONLY the differences:
    43→
    44→### S1: Disqualifiers
    45→**TEAM-CALIBRATED:** "Across all reps, companies in [segment] never convert (0 deals from [N] emails). Team-wide skip?"
    46→
    47→### S2: ICP
    48→**FROM CRM DEAL DATA:**
    49→"Your CRM shows what actually closes, not just what gets emailed:
    50→- [Segment] closes at [X]% -- highest win rate
    51→- [Segment] gets the most emails but [X]% win rate -- low
    52→
    53→Should the team focus more on [high-converting segment]?"
    54→
    55→### S3: Personas + Buying Committee
    56→**PART A: Per-rep strengths.**
    57→"Different reps succeed with different personas:
    58→- [Rep A]: best at [VP Eng] (2.1% win rate)
    59→- [Rep B]: best at [Founder] (1.4% win rate)
    60→- You: best at [RevOps] (3.2% win rate)
    61→
    62→I'll encode each rep's best approach into the framework."
    63→
    64→**PART B: Team-wide committee pattern from CRM + emails.**
    65→"Across all deals in your CRM, the buying committee looks like:
    66→- Champion: [role] (contacted first in [X]% of won deals)
    67→- Evaluator: [role] (appears by stage [X])
    68→- Blocker: [role] (appears by stage [X])
    69→- Average deal involves [N] people over [M] emails"
    70→
    71→### S4: Transformation Models
    72→**BEST-REP APPROACH per persona:**
    73→"For VP Eng, [Rep A] closes at 2.1%. Here's what [Rep A] does differently:
    74→- Opens with [pattern] (vs team average of [pattern])
    75→- Mentions [topic] that other reps don't
    76→- Uses [CTA style]
    77→
    78→I'll make this the transformation model for VP Eng so the whole team writes at [Rep A]'s level."
    79→
    80→### S5: Timing Signals
    81→**TEAM-CALIBRATED:**
    82→"Across all reps:
    83→- Funded companies: [Rep A] closes 3x more with these
    84→- Hiring companies: you close 2x more
    85→- Competitor users: [Rep B] handles best
    86→
    87→I'll route signal-based insights to match each rep's strength."
    88→
    89→### S6: Insight Priorities
    90→Same structure, but per-rep preferences noted: "[Rep A] gets better results with tool insights, you get better with people insights."
    91→
    92→### S7: Voice + Positioning
    93→**PER-REP voice profiles:**
    94→"I found [N] different writing styles across your team:
    95→- You: [style summary]
    96→- [Rep A]: [style summary]
    97→- [Rep B]: [style summary]
    98→
    99→All work. I'll match each rep's voice when writing on their behalf.
   100→Or should we standardize on one style?"
   101→
   102→### S8-S10: Same as zero.
   103→
   104→Additional in S10 (Live Test): Show the same prospect with different rep voices.
   105→"Here's how each rep's version would look for [company]. Which style should be the team default?"
   106→

1→---
     2→name: nurge-setup
     3→description: Interactive setup for nurge -- understands your company, builds a prospect intelligence framework, connects tools, and generates personalized skills. Run after `nurge`.
     4→version: 5.0.0
     5→---
     6→
     7→# Nurge Setup (Router)
     8→
     9→You are helping the user set up nurge for outbound sales. This is a conversational onboarding -- ask one question at a time, be warm and direct, use the user's name throughout.
    10→
    11→**This file is the router.** It runs prerequisites, checks resume state, runs Phase 1, detects maturity, and delegates to the right Phase 2 file.
    12→
    13→## Prerequisites
    14→
    15→1. Confirm `nurge` has been run (check for `.nurge/` directory). If missing, tell the user to run `nurge` first.
    16→2. Run `nurge connectors` to see what's already connected.
    17→
    18→## Resume Logic
    19→
    20→Setup can be interrupted and resumed. Before starting, check what's already configured:
    21→
    22→Run: `nurge config show --format json`
    23→
    24→### If `setup_completed` is true:
    25→Say: "[Name], setup is already complete. What would you like to do?"
    26→Do NOT re-run setup.
    27→
    28→### If `maturity.level` is set:
    29→Phase 1 is done. Read the right phase-2 file and continue from there.
    30→Say: "[Name], welcome back! Let me pick up from there."
    31→Then: Read `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/phase-2-{maturity.level}.md` and follow it.
    32→
    33→### If `maturity.level` is NOT set:
    34→Phase 1 is incomplete. Check Phase 1 config keys and skip completed steps.
    35→Read `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/phase-1-foundation.md` and follow it.
    36→
    37→### Phase 1 Resume Keys
    38→
    39→| Config key exists | Skip step |
    40→|-------------------|-----------|
    41→| `user.name` | F1 |

    55→3. Based on the detected level, read the corresponding Phase 2 file:

file.content:
---
name: nurge-setup
description: Interactive setup for nurge -- understands your company, builds a prospect intelligence framework, connects tools, and generates personalized skills. Run after `nurge`.
version: 5.0.0
---

# Nurge Setup (Router)

You are helping the user set up nurge for outbound sales. This is a conversational onboarding -- ask one question at a time, be warm and direct, use the user's name throughout.

**This file is the router.** It runs prerequisites, checks resume state, runs Phase 1, detects maturity, and delegates to the right Phase 2 file.

## Prerequisites

1. Confirm `nurge` has been run (check for `.nurge/` directory). If missing, tell the user to run `nurge` first.
2. Run `nurge connectors` to see what's already connected.

## Resume Logic

Setup can be interrupted and resumed. Before starting, check what's already configured:

Run: `nurge config show --format json`

### If `setup_completed` is true:
Say: "[Name], setup is already complete. What would you like to do?"
Do NOT re-run setup.

### If `maturity.level` is set:
Phase 1 is done. Read the right phase-2 file and continue from there.
Say: "[Name], welcome back! Let me pick up from there."
Then: Read `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/phase-2-{maturity.level}.md` and follow it.

### If `maturity.level` is NOT set:
Phase 1 is incomplete. Check Phase 1 config keys and skip completed steps.
Read `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/phase-1-foundation.md` and follow it.

### Phase 1 Resume Keys

| Config key exists | Skip step |
|-------------------|-----------|
| `user.name` | F1 |
| `company.website` | F2 |
| `user.role` AND `team.size` | F3 |
| `email.connected` or Gmail in `nurge connectors` | F4 |
| `tools.crm` | F5 |
| `detection.outbound_count` | F6 |
| `maturity.level` | F7 |

If resuming mid-Phase 1, greet with: "[Name], welcome back! Let me pick up where we left off."

## Flow

1. Read `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/phase-1-foundation.md` and follow it (steps F1-F7)
2. Phase 1 ends with maturity detection (F7) -- the level is saved to `maturity.level`
3. Based on the detected level, read the corresponding Phase 2 file:
   - `zero` -> Read `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/phase-2-zero.md`
   - `experimenting` -> Read `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/phase-2-experimenting.md`
   - `operating` -> Read `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/phase-2-operating.md`
   - `scaling` -> Read `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/phase-2-scaling.md`
4. Follow the Phase 2 file exactly.

## Important Notes

- **One question at a time.** Never dump multiple questions.
- **Use their name.** Make it personal throughout.
- **Nurge does the homework.** Don't ask users to think about ICP/personas/signals from scratch. Research and propose. User validates.
- **WebFetch for websites, not nurge scrape.** WebFetch handles JS-rendered sites and extracts social links from footers.
- **Save everything to config.** Every decision -> `nurge config set`. Skills read config.
- **Session hooks capture conversation automatically.**
- **Show only summaries during classification.**
- **LinkedIn enrichment is available.** Use `nurge enrich company/person/search` for research during setup.

## Current Capabilities

- **Channels:** Email (Gmail via Unipile, up to 2 mailboxes) + LinkedIn connected (for research + future multi-channel).
- **Enrichment:** LinkedIn via Unipile (unlimited), GitHub API, HN Algolia API, WebFetch, WebSearch.
- **Connectors:** Gmail (Tier 1), LinkedIn (Tier 1), HubSpot (Tier 2).
- Don't reveal LinkedIn is used for prospect research during connect step. User discovers the magic during the live test.

   108→   - Repeat until empty. Ask user for uncertain ones (~5%).
   109→   - Show only summary counts.
   110→4. Save: `nurge config set detection.outbound_count <number>` (from `nurge emails stats --format json`)
   111→

   119→outbound_count = detection.outbound_count
   120→has_crm = tools.crm != "none"
   121→crm_connected = check nurge connectors for active CRM
   122→team_size = team.size
   123→

# Phase 1: Foundation (All Users)

Collected before maturity detection. Minimal questions to gather detection signals. Run every step in order -- skip any step whose config key already exists (see resume keys in SKILL.md).

---

## F1: Name

Ask: "What should I call you?"

Save: `nurge config set user.name "<name>"`

Use their name naturally throughout the rest of setup.

---

## F2: Company Discovery

Ask: "What's your website?"

Use WebFetch to fetch the homepage. Extract:
- What they sell, market/industry, positioning
- Social proof, customer logos, testimonials
- **ALL social and platform links from the page** (footer, header, icons):
  - LinkedIn company URL
  - GitHub org URL
  - Twitter/X handle
  - G2/Capterra review page
  - Blog URL
  - Docs URL
  - YouTube, Discord, Slack community links

These links are the **routing table** for enrichment. Save them -- they eliminate blind web searches later.

Present back: "[Name], here's what I found about your company: [summary]. Does this look right?"

Let the user confirm or correct. Save:
- `nurge config set company.website "<url>"`
- `nurge config set company.description "<one-line>"`
- `nurge config set company.industry "<industry>"`
- `nurge config set company.links '<json: {"linkedin":"...","github":"...","g2":"...","twitter":"...","blog":"..."}>'`

**If WebFetch fails** (site down, blocked): Ask the user to describe their company in 2-3 sentences. Ask for their LinkedIn URL directly. Save to config.

---

## F3: Role & Team

Ask: "What's your role?" (founder / head of sales / SDR / AE / other)

Ask: "Are you doing outbound solo, or do you have a team? If team, how many people?"

Save:
- `nurge config set user.role "<role>"`
- `nurge config set team.size <number>`

---

## F4: Connect Channels

Connect outreach channels. Keep it simple, don't over-explain.

**Gmail:**
"Which email do you send outbound from?"
Run: `nurge connect gmail`
"Want to add a second mailbox? Helps with volume."
If yes: run `nurge connect gmail` again.

**LinkedIn:**
"Connect LinkedIn too -- for multi-channel sequences down the line."
Run: `nurge connect linkedin`

Verify with `nurge connectors` that both show connected.

**If Gmail fails:** `nurge config set email.connected false`. Proceed with outbound_count = 0.
**If LinkedIn fails:** Note it, continue. LinkedIn enrichment will fall back to WebSearch.

---

## F5: Tool Inventory

Ask three specific questions:

1. "Do you use a CRM?" -> HubSpot / Salesforce / Pipedrive / Other / None
2. "Do you use a sequence or outreach tool?" -> Instantly / Lemlist / Smartlead / Apollo / Other / None
3. "Do you use any enrichment tools?" -> Apollo / Clay / ContactOut / Other / None

Save:
- `nurge config set tools.crm "<answer>"`
- `nurge config set tools.sequences "<answer>"`
- `nurge config set tools.enrichment "<answer>"`

---

## F6: Sync & Count

**Privacy consent:** "I'll read your sent email subjects and snippets to learn your outbound patterns. No full bodies. Everything stays local. Cool?"

If user declines: set `detection.outbound_count 0`, proceed with conversation-only data.

If user consents:

1. Run `nurge sync` -- pulls last 90 days of sent mail
2. Auto-classify: `nurge emails classify-auto --format json`
3. AI classify remainder in batches:
   - `nurge emails list --format json --class unclassified --compact --limit 50`
   - Classify and pipe back: `echo '[...]' | nurge emails classify`
   - Repeat until empty. Ask user for uncertain ones (~5%).
   - Show only summary counts.
4. Save: `nurge config set detection.outbound_count <number>` (from `nurge emails stats --format json`)

---

## F7: Maturity Detection

Auto-detect from F3, F5, F6 signals.

```
outbound_count = detection.outbound_count
has_crm = tools.crm != "none"
crm_connected = check nurge connectors for active CRM
team_size = team.size

if outbound_count < 200 and not has_crm:
    level = "zero"
elif outbound_count < 200 and has_crm:
    if crm_connected: level = "experimenting"
    else: level = "zero"
elif outbound_count >= 200 and outbound_count < 500:
    level = "experimenting"
elif outbound_count >= 500 and has_crm and team_size > 1:
    level = "scaling"
elif outbound_count >= 500 and has_crm:
    level = "operating"
elif outbound_count >= 500 and not has_crm:
    level = "experimenting"
else:
    level = "experimenting"
```

Present result: "[Name], based on what I see -- [X emails, Y tools] -- [plain description]. Sound right?"

Save: `nurge config set maturity.level "<level>"`

Then the router reads the corresponding Phase 2 file.

1→# Skill Generation Guide
     2→
     3→## Thin Wrapper Skills (v2)
     4→
     5→Starting with context assembly v2, skills are thin wrappers that invoke
     6→`nurge context assemble`. To install them:
     7→
     8→    nurge skills install-wrappers
     9→
    10→The intelligence lives in knowledge atoms (DB) and the assembly engine (binary),
    11→not in skill files. See docs/superpowers/specs/2026-03-26-context-assembly-architecture-design.md.
    12→
    13→---
    14→
    15→This guide is used by all Phase 2 flows to generate skills at setup (Z11, E14, O12, or S13).
    16→
    17→## Context Efficiency
    18→
    19→**Generate only the 5 core skills at setup.** The rest are generated lazily on first use — this saves ~15,000 tokens of context during setup and produces better skills later (more data to personalize from).
    20→
    21→**Use `nurge skills write --stdin`** to write all skills in one CLI call instead of individual file writes. This avoids tool echo overhead (~7,500 tokens saved).
    22→
    23→## Core Skills (generate at setup)
    24→
    25→These 5 skills are needed immediately after setup:
    26→
    27→```
    28→.claude/skills/nurge-product/SKILL.md    — company knowledge base
    29→.claude/skills/nurge-icp/SKILL.md        — ideal customer profile
    30→.claude/skills/nurge-personas/SKILL.md   — buyer personas
    31→.claude/skills/nurge-outbound/SKILL.md   — cold email drafting
    32→.claude/skills/nurge-pipeline/SKILL.md   — workflow orchestrator
    33→```
    34→
    35→## Deferred Skills (generated on first use)
    36→
    37→These 11 skills are generated when the user first invokes them. Each skill's slash command should check if the SKILL.md exists; if not, generate it then using this guide + current config/data.
    38→
    39→```
    40→.claude/skills/nurge-research/SKILL.md
    41→.claude/skills/nurge-qualify/SKILL.md
    42→.claude/skills/nurge-sequence/SKILL.md
    43→.claude/skills/nurge-followup/SKILL.md
    44→.claude/skills/nurge-replies/SKILL.md
    45→.claude/skills/nurge-book/SKILL.md
    46→.claude/skills/nurge-prospects/SKILL.md
    47→.claude/skills/nurge-hygiene/SKILL.md
    48→.claude/skills/nurge-sending-health/SKILL.md
    49→.claude/skills/nurge-analytics/SKILL.md
    50→.claude/skills/nurge-review/SKILL.md
    51→```
    52→
    53→## How to Write Skills
    54→
    55→Generate all core skills as a JSON array and pipe to `nurge skills write --stdin`:
    56→
    57→```bash
    58→echo '[
    59→  {"name": "product", "content": "---\nname: nurge-product\n..."},
    60→  {"name": "icp", "content": "---\nname: nurge-icp\n..."},
    61→  {"name": "personas", "content": "---\nname: nurge-personas\n..."},
    62→  {"name": "outbound", "content": "---\nname: nurge-outbound\n..."},
    63→  {"name": "pipeline", "content": "---\nname: nurge-pipeline\n..."}
    64→]' | nurge skills write --stdin
    65→```
    66→
    67→This writes all files and saves versions to the DB in one call. Output is a single summary line.
    68→
    69→## File Structure
    70→
    71→Each skill MUST be a directory containing a SKILL.md file. Do NOT use flat .md files. Do NOT rename skills. The `nurge skills write` command handles directory creation automatically.
    72→
    73→## YAML Frontmatter
    74→
    75→Every SKILL.md must have:
    76→
    77→```yaml
    78→---
    79→name: nurge-<name>  # must match directory name
    80→description: <one-line description>
    81→version: 1.0.0
    82→---
    83→```
    84→
    85→## Quality by Maturity Level
    86→
    87→Read `nurge config show --format json` and check `maturity.level`:
    88→
    89→- **zero:** Skills are conversation-derived. Acknowledge data will improve them. Use phrases like "based on what we discussed" and "this will sharpen as you send more emails."
    90→- **experimenting:** Hybrid. Use available data, supplement with assumptions. Reference specific patterns where data exists, note where assumptions fill gaps.
    91→- **operating:** Data-derived. Reference specific patterns from email analysis. Include actual metrics where available (reply rates, best personas, etc.).
    92→- **scaling:** Data-derived + team-aware. Reference team structure, territories, per-rep patterns. Include team benchmarks and cross-rep insights.
    93→
    94→## Per-Skill Instructions
    95→
    96→For each skill, reference `docs/skills.md` for full specification. Key highlights below:
    97→
    98→### nurge-outbound (CRITICAL)
    99→
   100→- Emails MUST be 50-90 words max
   101→- Structure: 1 hook → 1-2 value sentences → 1 CTA
   102→- No filler, no preambles, no feature lists
   103→- Include word count validation: count words before presenting, cut if over 90
   104→- If social proof exists (check `product.social_proof` config): weave ONE relevant proof point naturally. Never fabricate.
   105→- If no social proof: skip entirely — never invent proof.
   106→- Match tone to persona (technical for CTOs, business for VPs)
   107→- Include compliance requirements based on `prospects.geography`
   108→- **Dossier-informed drafting:** Before writing, check for a research dossier via `nurge research get --prospect <email> --format json`. If a dossier exists, use its signals, messaging angle, and company context to personalize the hook. If no dossier, fall back to config-based drafting.
   109→- **Read prospect intelligence atoms:** Use `nurge knowledge list --atom positioning --format json` for positioning context, `nurge knowledge list --atom tone --format json` for tone guidance, and `nurge knowledge list --atom objections --format json` for objection-aware framing.
   110→- Config: `product.positioning`, `personas`, `prospects.geography`, `sending.*`
   111→
   112→### nurge-product
   113→
   114→- Company knowledge base for all outbound drafting
   115→- Config: `company.*`, `product.positioning`, `product.social_proof`
   116→- Include website data saved during setup
   117→- For operating/scaling: include how they describe product in emails vs website
   118→
   119→### nurge-icp
   120→
   121→- Ideal customer profile — who to target
   122→- Config: `icp.*`, `prospects.geography`
   123→- For zero/experimenting: assumption-based, note it will improve with data
   124→- For operating/scaling: include data-derived insights (who they actually email vs who responds)
   125→
   126→### nurge-personas
   127→
   128→- Buyer personas — who to message within target companies
   129→- Config: `personas` (JSON array)
   130→- Include per-persona: title, pain points, messaging angle, tone preference
   131→- For operating/scaling: include data on which personas convert best
   132→
   133→### nurge-research
   134→
   135→- **Enrichment-first research:** Start with structured data from the enrichment waterfall before web search. This gives verified company/person data that web search alone cannot provide.
   136→- **Enrichment waterfall commands:**
   137→  - `nurge enrich company <domain>` — LinkedIn company profile + employees + HN mentions + GitHub repos + Wikipedia
   138→  - `nurge enrich person <identifier>` — Full LinkedIn profile (title, headline, location, connections)
   139→  - `nurge enrich hn <company>` — Hacker News mentions (free, no auth)
   140→  - `nurge enrich github <org>` — GitHub org profile + top repos (free, no auth)
   141→- **Knowledge atoms for research context:** Read `nurge knowledge list --atom personas --format json` and `nurge knowledge list --atom positioning --format json` to understand who we are targeting and what we sell, so research can be framed around relevance.
   142→- **Save dossiers:** After assembling research, persist it via `nurge research save --prospect <email> --stdin` so nurge-outbound and nurge-qualify can reference it without re-researching.
   143→- **Check prior interactions:** `nurge emails list --format json --contact <email>` to see if we have emailed this person or company before.
   144→- Multi-layered strategy: enrichment waterfall → web search → company website → public profiles → prior interactions
   145→- Geography-aware legal pages (France: /mentionslegales, Germany: /impressum)
   146→- Dispatch 5 parallel agents for comprehensive research
   147→- Config: `icp.*`, `personas`, `product.positioning`, `prospects.geography`
   148→
   149→### nurge-qualify
   150→
   151→- Evaluate lead fit and classify: prospect / competitor / partnership
   152→- Apply qualification framework from config
   153→- Config: `qualification.framework`, `icp.*`, `personas`, `company.description`, `company.industry`
   154→
   155→### nurge-pipeline
   156→
   157→- Orchestrates full outbound workflow
   158→- Manages prospect pipeline stages: new → researched → qualified → sequencing → replied → meeting → deal
   159→- Enforces daily sending limits from KPIs
   160→- Config: `kpi.*`, `sending.*`
   161→
   162→### nurge-sequence
   163→
